 o   those   who   may   approach   the   present   work   from
 a philosophical   and   historical   standpoint   the presence   of its

insburg’s   destructive   criticism   there   has   been   no   attention

 ithin   the   region   of research   which   has   only   a   scholastic
horizon—whether   metaphysical   or   historical—there   was   no
interest   that   calls   for   special   mention   at   the   period   which
preceded   that   work   immediately,   and   it is quite   true   that
 there has been as little subsequently   to its publication, but not
on   account   of   r.   insburg’s   criticism.   here   has   been
always   or,   speaking   eadtly,   since   the   days   of obert   ludd
and   homas   aughan,   of   udworth   and   the   ambridge
 latonists,   a   certain   class   of students   for   whom   the   claims
 made   by   and   on   behalf   of   the   abbalah   have   possessed
 importance,   and   this class is possibly larger now   than   at any
 date   prior to   .   t forms   also intellectually   a more   con-
 siderable   body than the academical   reader   might   be disposed
to imagine   in the absence   of particular acquaintance   with the
 literature by which it is represented.   ne is obliged to speak
 of   these   students   and   thinkers   under   the   designation   of
 theosophical   or esoteric   groups,   though   the phrase   is ineact
and has been used   to describe   persons   who   have   little title to
consideration.    proscribed   mode   of thought   is here, as in
 other   cases,   identified   with   the lesser capacities   that follow   it,
 and   what   was   once   an   unpopular   subje&   has   been   classed
 according   to the waste   and drift which   has colle&ed   about   it.
 ut   the   class   to   which    have   adverted   does   not   in   itself
 deserve   either ridicule or contempt    it is that which   believes

mystical radition   from   an early period of human   history, and
this is not manifestly an absurd or unwarrantable   consequence

 to draw from the study of religions undertaken   in a compara-
tive sense.   t is a question of evidence   and should   be left to

suppose,   but   it is   true   as   a fad,   that   to   theosophists   and
 mystics,   more   than   all,   if not   unto   these   eclusively,   an
 inquiry   like   the   present   must   appeal.   ther   interests   are

 moment   regarding   it.
